title: China to miss League of Legends at 2026 Asian Games
summary: China to miss League of Legends at 2026 Asian Games  Sheep Esports
content:
The competitive League of Legends ecosystem is buzzing following the latest announcement regarding 'China to miss League of Legends at 2026 Asian Games'. As the season progresses, this development has quickly taken center stage, drawing intense scrutiny from fans, analysts, and professional players alike. The core update highlights a significant shift that is expected to have wide-ranging consequences for the regional leagues and international tournaments.

Riot Games continues to refine the competitive structure, balancing the game's high skill ceiling with spectator entertainment. The professional scene is an ever-evolving battleground where even the smallest adjustment can make or break a team's championship aspirations. Every coach, analyst, and player must stay ahead of the curve to remain competitive.

Speaking on the recent events, seasoned esports analysts noted that 'China to miss League of Legends at 2026 Asian Games  Sheep Esports' is not just a temporary trend but a fundamental shift in how teams will approach their upcoming matchdays. 'Draft priority and map control are going to be completely redefined over the next few weeks,' remarked a prominent league caster. 'Teams that fail to recognize the gravity of this change will find themselves falling behind very quickly in the standings.'

As the community awaits the upcoming weekend matches, anticipation continues to build. Fans can catch all the high-stakes action live on official streaming channels, complete with expert desk analysis and live regional broadcasts. With so much on the line, the next chapter of the 2026 competitive season promises to deliver some of the most memorable moments in League of Legends esports history.

title: Hanwha Life Esports Secures 1st Place in 2026 LCK Rounds 1 & 2... Faces T1 on the 12th
summary: Hanwha Life Esports Secures 1st Place in 2026 LCK Rounds 1 & 2... Faces T1 on the 12th  Inven Global
content:
The 2026 LCK regular season concluded its nine-week run for Rounds 1 and 2. In a final week where the top spot remained undecided until the very end, Hanwha Life Esports swept Hanjin Brion 2-0 to overtake T1 and Gen.G, clinching first place.

Riot Games, the organizer of the League of Legends Champions Korea (LCK)—the Korean professional league for League of Legends (LoL)—announced that the ninth week of the 2026 LCK regular season concluded on Sunday the 31st, following matches held from Wednesday the 27th at LoL PARK in Jongno-gu, Seoul.

Hanwha Life Esports claimed the final throne for the first and second rounds. With a 2-0 victory over Hanjin Brion on the final day of the second round, they finished the season with a 15-3 record.

By holding off T1 and Gen.G, who were chasing the top spot until the final moments, Hanwha Life Esports secured a berth in the third round of the LCK MSI representative qualifiers. T1 also finished the ninth week with a two-game winning streak, ending with a 14-4 record; while tied with Gen.G in wins and losses, T1 took second place due to a superior set point differential.

Consequently, Hanwha Life Esports and T1 will face off in the third-round No. 1 seed decider on the 12th at the Wonju DB Promy Arena. The winner will immediately secure a spot in the 2026 MSI, which will be held in Daejeon. Gen.G, finishing third, will wait in the fourth round, while fourth-place kt Rolster will begin the qualifiers from the second round at LoL PARK.

Hanjin Brion claimed the final ticket to the MSI qualifiers. After languishing at the bottom of the standings with a six-game losing streak early in the season, they staged a dramatic turnaround to finish in sixth place. Based on the final standings, a total of six teams—Hanwha Life Esports, T1, Gen.G, kt Rolster, Dplus KIA, and Hanjin Brion—will compete in the qualifiers for a chance to represent the LCK at the MSI.

title: Celebrate Pride 2026!
content:
We’re back after a year of new TFT sets, a Darkin entering the Rift, and so much more! And through whatever highs and lows that have come and gone for you this year, we hope that League of Legends and Teamfight Tactics have been a welcoming place to be you, play with friends, and make sick plays (ofc). 2026 also marks our sixth year celebrating Pride and our ninth year supporting, with in-game Icons, homeguard trails, emotes, and more. For this year, we’re back with some freebies, but if you wanna know more about what Riot Games as a company and brand is doing, be sure to check out the Riot blog releasing June 1st! Past that, be sure to check out our social channels during June as we’re working with a few awesome LGBTQ+ artists for a few pieces being released throughout the month.

For the rest of this piece, we’ll be going over the in-game missions and free content dropping into League and TFT on May 28th just after both games patch.

League of Legends kicks things off with the launch of Patch 26.11, with an event that will go till Patch 26.13 ends. On the Rift, we’ve got two new Pride emotes and the return of all our past Pride content. That means homeguards, emotes, and icons from previous years.

These three definitely know how to get through a teamfight together.

For those who need to hear it, there’s a rainbow fish in the sea just waiting to be caught by you.

Both emotes will be available in the store for 1 Blue Essence.There will also be a mission to help you advance your Battle Pass, obtainable by joining in the prideful fun with friends. It’s worth noting that this mission will only be active for the first patch of Pride, 26.11.

title: LEC Spring 2026: Team Vitality and Natus Vincere knocked out, only four teams remain
